Time Series Forecasting and Analysis Report

Forecasting Framework

2026-06-14

Executive Summary

This document provides a comprehensive analysis and forecast of the time series dataset loaded from data/daily-total-female-births.csv.

The analysis steps include: 1. Data cleaning and preparation. 2. Exploratory time series analysis (stationarity, decomposition, autocorrelation). 3. Model fitting (Seasonal Naive, ETS, Holt-Winters, and Auto-ARIMA). 4. Validation and error metrics evaluation against a test holdout set. 5. Statistical diagnostics of residuals. 6. Multi-step ahead forecasting.

1. Data Exploration & Quality Checks

# Ensure data is present
if (params$data_path == "data/daily-total-female-births.csv") {
  download_default_data(params$data_path)
}
## [1] TRUE
raw_df <- load_and_clean_data(params$data_path)
y <- create_ts_object(raw_df, frequency = params$frequency)

# Basic statistics
summary_stats <- summary(y)

The time series contains 365 observations. Below is the statistical summary of the values:

Time Series Descriptive Statistics
Min 1st Qu. Median Mean 3rd Qu. Max
23 37 42 41.98082 46 73

1.1 Visualizing the Time Series

The raw observations plotted over time to check for trends, seasonality, or obvious outliers:

1.2 Stationarity Test

We perform the Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test to evaluate if the series is stationary. - Null Hypothesis (\(H_0\)): The series has a unit root (is non-stationary). - Alternative Hypothesis (\(H_a\)): The series is stationary.

adf_res <- adf.test(y)

The ADF statistic is -5.1042 with a p-value of 0.01. Since the p-value is less than 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that the series is stationary.

1.3 Time Series Decomposition

Applying Seasonal and Trend decomposition using LOESS (STL) to separate the series into its underlying component parts: trend, weekly seasonal pattern, and residual noise.

2. Model Fitting and Evaluation

We split the dataset into training and test holdout sets using a split ratio of 70%. - Training Set Size: 255 observations. - Test Set Size (Forecast Horizon \(h\)): 110 observations.

split <- split_train_test(y, train_ratio = params$train_ratio)
train <- split$train
test <- split$test
h <- split$h

We fit four forecasting models to the training data: 1. Seasonal Naive (SNAIVE): Projects the last observed seasonal cycle forward. 2. ETS (Error, Trend, Seasonal): State space exponential smoothing model. 3. Holt-Winters (HW): Triple exponential smoothing. 4. Auto-ARIMA (ARIMA): Automated Box-Jenkins modeling.

fc_snaive <- fit_snaive_model(train, h = h)
fc_ets <- fit_ets_model(train, h = h)
fc_hw <- fit_holtwinters_model(train, h = h, seasonal = "additive")
fc_arima <- fit_arima_model(train, h = h)

2.1 Model Comparison & Accuracy Metrics

We compute the prediction errors on the test dataset.

Model Accuracy Comparison on Test Holdout Set
ME RMSE MAE MPE MAPE MASE
SNAIVE -1.118 9.514 7.627 -5.062 17.745 1.049
ETS 1.087 7.277 5.681 -0.007 12.737 0.781
HoltWinters -7.934 12.166 9.995 -21.016 24.601 1.375
ARIMA 0.790 7.262 5.602 -0.772 12.620 0.771

2.2.4 Auto-ARIMA Forecast

3. Residual Diagnostics (Auto-ARIMA)

A good model should have residuals that resemble white noise (uncorrelated, zero mean, constant variance). We examine the residuals of the Auto-ARIMA model.

3.1 Portmanteau Test (Ljung-Box)

We run a Ljung-Box test to check if the residuals have significant autocorrelations. - Null Hypothesis (\(H_0\)): Residuals are independent (no autocorrelation, white noise). - Alternative Hypothesis (\(H_a\)): Residuals exhibit autocorrelation.

lb_test <- Box.test(residuals(fc_arima$model), lag = params$frequency * 2, type = "Ljung-Box")
lb_test
## 
##  Box-Ljung test
## 
## data:  residuals(fc_arima$model)
## X-squared = 9.8282, df = 14, p-value = 0.7746

The Ljung-Box test p-value is 0.7746. Since the p-value is greater than 0.05, we fail to reject the null hypothesis, indicating that the residuals are uncorrelated and consistent with white noise.

4. Final Forecast

Based on the metrics above, the best-performing model can be selected. The following table displays the final forecast points and confidence bands for the upcoming 30 time periods.
